title: The Valence Table
blurb: The periodic table as a charge machine: an element's group sets its common ion charge, and a cation and anion combine in whatever ratio makes the compound neutral.
group_charge_note: Main-group metals lose electrons to a full shell (group 1 → +1, group 2 → +2); nonmetals gain them (group 16 → −2, group 17 → −1). Charges below are sourced, not inferred — the pattern is a guide, the data is the authority.

panel.pattern: Main-group metals form cations, nonmetals anions: group 1 → +1, group 2 → +2, group 13 → +3; group 15 → −3, group 16 → −2, group 17 → −1. The noble gases form no simple ion.
panel.why: Atoms bond toward the nearest noble-gas electron count. A metal sheds its few valence electrons to expose a full inner shell; a nonmetal gains the few it lacks — the charge is just how many electrons moved.
panel.exceptions: Transition metals (Fe and Cu here) hold more than one common charge — the Stock numeral in a name says which. Hydrogen sits in group 1 but is no metal; it shares electrons more often than it transfers them.
panel.where: Formula writing (charge crossover), nomenclature (iron(II) vs iron(III)), and predicting the products of double replacement.

panel.pattern: The count repeats across each row: 1 and 2 on the left (s-block), then 3 through 8 on the right (p-block). Elements in the same group carry the same count.
panel.why: Group position IS the outer-shell electron count for main-group elements — the table was arranged so that recurring chemistry lines up in columns, and the recurring thing is the outer shell.
panel.exceptions: Helium has 2, not 8 — its first shell fills at two electrons. The d-block count is convention-dependent, so it is left blank here rather than asserted.
panel.where: Ion charges (lose the count, or gain to reach 8), Lewis structures later, and why period-neighbors differ while group-neighbors rhyme.

panel.pattern: Rises left → right across a period, falls down a group — fluorine tops the scale at 3.98.
panel.why: Across a period the nuclear charge grows while electrons enter the same shell, so the pull on a shared pair strengthens. Down a group the outer shell sits farther out, screened by more inner shells, so the pull weakens.
panel.exceptions: Undefined for the noble gases on the Pauling scale — shown blank, never zero. The values are a fitted scale, not measured constants; other scales order a few neighbors differently.
panel.where: Bond polarity (the bonding mode's ΔEN), why oxygen and fluorine hog electron density, and acid-strength stories later.

panel.pattern: Shrinks left → right across a period, grows down a group — the biggest atoms sit bottom-left.
panel.why: Across a period, more protons pull the same shells in tighter. Down a group, each row adds a whole new shell outside the last.
panel.exceptions: A radius depends on how it is measured — these are single-bond covalent radii (Cordero 2008). Transition-metal values depend on spin state and are left blank rather than asserted.
panel.where: Which-atom-is-larger drills, bond-length estimates, and the size story underneath ionization energy and electronegativity.

panel.pattern: Rises left → right across a period, falls down a group — helium is the hardest atom in this set to ionize.
panel.why: The same pull that shrinks atoms holds their electrons: more nuclear charge on the same shell makes removal costlier; a farther, better-screened shell makes it cheaper.
panel.exceptions: Two famous dips break the climb: boron below beryllium (the lone p electron is easier to remove than a paired s), and oxygen below nitrogen (the first paired p electron repels its roommate). Both are visible in this data.
panel.where: Why metals give up electrons so readily, the order-by-ionization-energy drills, and why successive ionizations jump at shell boundaries (later).

mistake.note: Each ion's own charge became its own subscript — but 3×(+3) + 1×(−1) = +8, not 0. CROSS the charges: each subscript is the other ion's charge.

bonding.caution: A general guide with many exceptions — OpenStax's own words. HF sits near the ionic line yet is a polar covalent molecule, and some metal–nonmetal pairs with modest ΔEN are ionic anyway. The better guide is the atoms themselves: two nonmetals share electrons (covalent); a metal with a nonmetal usually transfers them (ionic).

classes.label: pure (nonpolar) covalent
classes.description: The atoms pull electrons about equally, so the pair is shared nearly evenly.

classes.label: polar covalent
classes.description: The pair is shared, but pulled toward the more electronegative atom — a bond dipole (δ+ / δ−).

classes.label: ionic
classes.description: The pull is lopsided enough that electron transfer is the better model — ions, not a shared pair.
